attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old man is admitted to the ward following open reduction and internal fixation of a right-sided tibial shaft fracture, after being involved in a motor vehicle accident. Eight hours post-surgery, he has been given four doses of morphine, and says he is still in significant pain. He also mentions that he has a "funny tingling sensation" in his right leg, which is not present in the left leg. He has been mobile multiple times since the surgery.

His past medical history includes cellulitis which previously required hospital admission and and "pain in his calves when he walks", which subsides with rest.

On examination, the patient is obviously in significant discomfort. Femoral, popliteal and dorsal pedis pulses are all present in the right leg. The patient experiences significant pain with passive movement of the right leg. The left leg examines normally.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
This patients' symptoms are most consistent with a diagnosis of compartment syndrome. The classical 'six P's' of compartment syndrome are: pain (out of proportion to injury), pallor, pulselessness, paraesthesia, paralysis, and poikilothermia. Paraesthesia and disproportionate pain are early signs of compartment syndrome, while pulselessness is a late sign of compartment syndrome. Urgent fasciotomy is the definitive management for compartment syndrome, as it relieves the pressure which has built up in the affected compartment.

Angiography and urgent thrombolysis would not be appropriate in this scenario. While the patient has a prior diagnosis of intermittent claudication, it is unlikely that he has a deep venous thrombosis or has suddenly developed critical limb ischaemia secondary to this condition. While DVTs are commonly seen in the post-operative period, they more commonly occur around day five in patients who have been immobile.

IV Antibiotics would be appropriate if this patient was presenting with symptoms consistent with severe cellulitis of the affected limb. However, cellulitis usually presents as a painful, red, hot and swollen area of skin over a particular limb (most commonly the lower limbs), rather than pain and paraesthesia.

Leg elevation would not be appropriate in this setting, and would most likely only demonstrate position-dependent pallor and rubor consistent with his previous diagnosis of intermittent claudication.
learningPoints: Breakthrough pain which does not respond to analgesia should prompt consideration of compartment syndrome
Pulselessness is a late sign of compartment syndrome, which indicates a poor prognosis
references: Garner MR, Taylor SA, Gausden E, Lyden JP. Compartment syndrome: diagnosis, management, and unique concerns in the twenty-first century. HSS Journal®. 2014 Jul;10(2):143-52.
